beats.text: Here's matrix A — entries two, one, three, four — sitting next to the target vector five, eleven.

Cramer's Rule: want the first unknown? Swap column one with the target. The new matrix shows five, one, eleven, four. Its determinant: five times four minus one times eleven — that's nine. Divide by det(A) — two times four minus one times three — equals five. First unknown: nine over five.

Same idea powers the inverse. A-inverse equals one over det(A), times the adjugate — the transpose of the cofactor matrix. For this two-by-two: flip the diagonal to four, two, swap signs on the off-diagonal to negative three, two, scale everything by one-fifth.

Each cofactor is the determinant left behind after crossing out one row and one column. A highlight marks the eliminated row and column, leaving a smaller matrix whose determinant feeds back into the whole formula.

Cramer's Rule is elegant — but computing determinants scales at factorial speed. For large systems, elimination wins. These formulas reveal the geometry: det(A) measures area, volume, and exactly when the inverse breaks down.
